Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores since I learn best by hearing things. I aimed for maybe 10 hours a week but spread it out whenever I had free moments. With 19 online courses to juggle, I couldn't really dedicate specific blocks to just one class. I'd batch similar content together and just power through what needed to get done.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I didn't have a strict schedule because life doesn't really work that way for me. When deadlines piled up, I'd focus on whatever was most urgent. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then cram a bunch of content before an assignment was due. It wasn't ideal but it's how I managed everything.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment deadlines or submit something. Sometimes I'd get a notification about new content being posted. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges or points. But honestly, unless something specific prompted me, I wouldn't just browse around the LMS for fun.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. The gamified elements helped a bit because checking my streaks or leaderboard position became mildly interesting. But with so many courses, I couldn't make any single platform feel routine. I'd go in bursts rather than maintaining consistent engagement.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded discussions were gold for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could download them and listen anywhere without needing to stare at a screen. The discussion forums were also useful because hearing different perspectives through written conversations helped concepts click. The gamified task system made completing work feel less like a chore and more like progress I could see.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and PDFs most of the time. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on screen for hours just doesn't work for me. I'd skim them at best or find audio alternatives elsewhere. The video content I watched extensively, but dense text-based resources got pushed aside unless absolutely necessary for an assignment.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or if it had some engaging element like the gamified tasks. If I could listen to it while doing something else, even better. Activities that felt like busywork or repetitive reading just made me zone out. I needed to feel like I was actually gaining something useful, not just checking boxes.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford with 19 courses demanding attention. Required stuff got priority because it directly impacted my grades. The optional content was probably valuable, but I had to triage constantly. If an optional activity had gamification elements or promised quick wins, I might engage with it, but otherwise it fell off my radar pretty quickly.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could and studied offline constantly. I'd save audio files to my phone and listen during my commute or while working. I also used free resources from YouTube and podcasts that explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me. A lot of my actual learning happened completely outside the LMS environment.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to downloaded lectures for hours while walking around or doing household tasks. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also discuss concepts with friends over messaging apps, which helped me understand things better. My LMS activity looked minimal but I was genuinely processing and engaging with the material in my own way.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd access something once, download it if possible, then never open it again on the platform. The video watch time probably shows some of this, but a lot of audio content I converted and took with me. I learned the material, just not in a way the system could track.
Q12: The course got harder to engage with when the content felt repetitive or when I was juggling deadlines across multiple courses. Sometimes boredom just set in because the material didn't challenge me in interesting ways. My attendance was basically non-existent because live sessions didn't fit my schedule, and I could learn the same content more efficiently on my own time.
Q13: Definitely. Being stretched across 19 courses with limited financial resources meant I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Fatigue was real, especially toward the end of the semester. When I was tired, I'd default to passive listening rather than active engagement. The LMS got less attention when life demanded more from me elsewhere.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions because I felt like I should be doing something, but my brain wasn't actually engaged. The gamified elements helped reduce this, but it still happened.
Q15: I definitely postponed studying for exams even though I knew they were important. Assignments I could handle because they were more practical and I could work on them in chunks. But sitting down to actually prepare for a test felt overwhelming with everything else going on. I kept telling myself I'd get to it, then ran out of time. That's probably why my exam score was so low despite decent assignment work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd check the LMS specifically to see what was due and when. Everything got prioritized by deadline proximity. Without a deadline looming, I probably wouldn't engage much with new content. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines and goals that kept me coming back more regularly.
Q17: Feedback did affect me when I actually received it. Good feedback on assignments motivated me to maintain that quality. But with so many courses, feedback was sometimes delayed or generic, which made it feel less impactful. When instructors acknowledged specific things I did well, I felt more connected to the course and more likely to engage actively.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I needed, but maybe too much flexibility. Without required attendance, I just didn't attend. The assignment-heavy approach worked well for me since I could complete those on my own schedule. But the structure didn't push me toward exam preparation, which clearly hurt my final performance.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier because they gave me small wins and visible progress. Having downloadable content was crucial for my learning style and situation. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the lack of structure pushing me toward consistent engagement. The course assumed I'd be self-motivated to attend and prepare for exams, which didn't happen.
Q20: I think that happens when students are exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or maybe procrastinating by browsing instead of working. They might also be looking for specific information repeatedly because they're confused. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes clicking around feels like doing something when you're actually avoiding the real work.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My LMS engagement looked low, but I was learning through downloaded materials and external resources. Some students are just efficient—they get what they need quickly and study elsewhere. The system can't see offline learning or alternative study methods, so low activity might hide real engagement happening outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students access materials and how often they complete tasks. That's useful for identifying who's completely absent. But logs miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and whether someone actually understood what they accessed. They can't capture listening to downloaded audio or discussing concepts with peers outside the system.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and connecting it to things I already know or care about. It's not just consuming content but processing it actively. For me, engagement happens when I'm curious about something or when completing a task feels rewarding, like with the gamified elements. It's a mental state, not just a behavior that can be logged.
Q24: My whole auditory learning approach wouldn't show up at all. The hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing other things, the conversations with classmates over text, the external resources I used—none of that appears in the data. Also, my high resilience and generally good mood meant I wasn't struggling emotionally even when my engagement looked low. I was managing fine, just not in visible ways.
